sentences.text: I was living in Antwerp, 48 [NON-ENGLISH] Street.
And when we heard the trucks and they stopped, we were wondering why they stopped.
Now, I had seen them from 1940 to 1942.
I saw SS people in my house.
And I saw a lot of German trucks.
And I was used to all of this, seeing it, but not at night, the trucks-- the trucks stopping.
They stopped.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we heard shouting, screaming.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my daddy got up from the table.
And he pulled a little bit the drape-- and aside.
And he said, oh, my god, this is it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In Yiddish, he said it.
I'm saying it in English.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
And then he let us all, one at a time, look out for a minute.
What did I see?
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I saw German soldiers [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] carrying, in their hands, flames, like they were making a movie, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] going from door to door, knocking at doors.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Those people who did not open a door, they broke the door.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And then I saw people in the street.
And I heard, I want to see my mommy!
Where is my mommy?
Please don't pull her by the hair.
She's paralyzed.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Daddy, where are you?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Please, don't kick her.
She's pregnant.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I heard many more things, shouting-- and seeing chaos.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I knew that we had a paralyzed woman in the street.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Some of the voices [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] were my friends' voices.
I could hear them-- or their parents' voices.
I still hear them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When they got to our door, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my daddy pointed at me.
And he said, go and open the door, Clara.
And I did.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I guess my daddy wanted me to open the door because he didn't-- he thought that they won't harm a little girl.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
But I opened the door.
And husky soldiers-- the Germans, with their black uniforms, with their boots, with their guns, with their clubs-- gave me a push.
And in German, they said, 'Jude?' [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And of course, I don't know why they even said it.
I-- they knew who they had.
And they came into my daddy's and to my dining room.
When they walked in, they asked my parents for their passport.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When my parents took out their passport-- and I have to admit, I always repeat that because, to me, it was a miracle.
I didn't even know my parents were carrying passports with them.
But when they took out the passport and showed it to them, the Germans looked at each other, and they said, tonight-- remember that word, 'tonight'-- we're not taking Romanian Jews.
This time-- my father had left Romania because of anti-Semitism.
This time, he was saved because he had a Romanian passport.
But tonight, not tomorrow night.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were in our house.
Our house was a three-story home.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And on the third floor, my daddy had a diamond cutter who lived there, Mrs.--
Chaimowitz-- a blank.
What a man.
And Mr.
Chaimowitz lived there with a little girl and a little boy.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
The Germans made me take them up.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Walking up, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they made me go first.
Why?
Because they wanted me to-- they wanted to be able to give me a good push, take out their gun and put it near my neck and say, do you want to be killed?
And all kinds of things they did until I got to the third floor.
When we got up there, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we opened the door.
And I look around.
And I don't see the little girl.
I just see the little boy.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And the Germans, they never talked.
They shouted when it came to the Jews.
And they started shouting, is this your family?
In German, of course-- is this your family?
And Mr.
Chaimowitz said, yeah.
And they said, where's your little girl?
You had a little girl.
And he said, they took her yesterday in the street.

What the Germans used to do, by the way-- one of the things too, when they did systematically, is if they decided to go with a truck on a few blocks and watch somebody go with a star, they just picked it up, put them in the truck, and that person never got back home-- a young child, an adult, whoever.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
They looked around.
And then they looked at me.
And they shouted at me.
And they said, are they telling the truth?
They didn't say it like I'm saying it.
They shouted at me.
I don't know what made me do what I did.
But I said, yes.
Now I knew that they had that little girl hidden in the room because I had seen that little girl a few minutes before, or a half hour before.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They looked around again.
And if they would have looked another about five minutes, they would have found her.
Because the apartment wasn't so big.
And they left with Mr.
And Mrs.
Chaimowitz and their son.
And their names, too, are in Auschwitz.
The little girl came down after we closed the door [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and was speechless.
I think she was seven, eight.
And my mother-- she was sitting on the steps.
And my mother said to her, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you want food?
And she nodded.
She never said a word.
That night-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when it became light in the morning-- of course, we didn't sleep that night.
But when it became light, we looked out.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we talk a lot about [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] things that happen in the world-- a fire, a flood, all kinds of things.
This wasn't the fire.
This wasn't a flood.
This was a street where dogs and cats and flowerpots were outside.
The street had a [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] path where they pulled the woman who was paralyzed-- of blood.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And there were no more Jews in that street.
The only ones was the Heller family.
[PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] The-- we, the Heller family, sat quietly, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] looked out, walked in, walked out.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We, too, couldn't speak.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We, too, were speechless.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
I'm sure that what my parents were thinking and what I was thinking were two different ways, two different things.
I was thinking of my friends.
Where are they?
I'm sure my mother and father were concerned-- how are they going to save their children's lives?

[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Around 6 o'clock in the morning, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] my sister's music teacher-- blank again-- [LAUGHS] [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Mr.-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Yeager, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] who was a German, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] came into our home [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and said the following.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I'm going to try and save your life.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You have 10 minutes to take something in your hands.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then you will leave the house.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
My mother and father said to us, did you hear?
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We all went into our rooms.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And each one of us took something in our hands.
Because if we would have taken luggage, the Germans would have seen that we are running from our home.
I took my photo album-- that we will see pictures later on.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My sister took some of her music.
My sister had a beautiful coloratura soprano.
My daddy took his tallit, his prayer shawl, and the Bible.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And each one of us took something.
And we left our home, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [CRYING] never to see it again.
[CRYING] Where did he take us?
He couldn't-- he told us not to-- he's not going to take us to his home because his wife hates Jews.
He took us to a bakery basement.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We went down to that basement.
And he said, you have to stay here as long as I want you to, until I find the place where you can hide.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, I want you to imagine that bakery.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] It was nothing but sacks-- stacks of sacks of flour-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no water in there, no toilets, no food, nothing.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I guess we all-- I'm sure I did, because I remember, when I got up, what happened to me-- we fell asleep.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When I got up-- my mother, before we left, said take two clothes-- two pieces of clothes on you.
Put it on.
And I put my coat on.
And when I got up, all my pockets for my clothes were full of mice.
They were crawling all over me.
And I wanted to shout.
And my mother put her hand on me.
And she said, the mice won't kill you.
If the Germans hear you, they will.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I stopped.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That basement had a little window, like a lot of basements do, even now.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We took turns to look out.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I looked out, standing on a sack of flour.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I saw a little boy [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] running with a bread in his hands, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and the Germans in back of him, shouting, halt!
Halt!
Stop!
Stop!
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The child did not stop.
The Germans shot the child in the back.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't know who this child was.
But I'm sure if I would have seen his face, I would have recognized him-- must have been from our neighborhood.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Nobody walked over to help that child.
I don't-- I'm sure that the child died.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] We sat down in the basement.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Everybody looked out, I guess.
And everybody felt different.
Nobody said a word.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My mother [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] said to us, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we're still alive.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So let's try and live.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We waited in the basement for two, three days.
My daddy finally found in the hallway some water.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I think that he took a chance and walked in, in the store, because he brought us back not cake, a piece of bread.
I remember it very well.
It was so big, maybe as big as my hand.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we waited for Mr.
Yeager to come back.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When he came back, he said, I found a place for you.
But you can't go in the daytime.
We will have to go at night.
And he brought us to a place, a family, Catholic family, the Adams, where we were in hiding there for three months or so.
That family-- first, I want to make sure that the world understands that no matter what we gave that family, they gave us more.
They gave us life.
They hid us.
And they put their life in danger.
They were Catholics.
They had six children.
And I will speak about one of them who just sent me a Christmas card that I have.
And we will show it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were in one little room.
We weren't allowed to go out.
And in the basement-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I should have said it-- started our wandering for nearly two and a half years in hiding where we did not see the daylight.
We did not have fresh air.
We did not have time to breathe because we were thinking and listening if the Germans are coming for us.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] But we were alive.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Some days, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] couldn't think.
It was like a black blanket in front of me.
I was living, but I wasn't existing.
Or I was existing, but not living.
I remember very well sitting for hours and hours and not saying anything.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The very difficult thing is that I would love-- would have loved to remember, but I guess I blanked it out-- what I did all those two and a half years, all those hours, all those minutes, all those months.
But I can't remember.
I do remember some things.
But for so much, I don't remember a lot.
Because I must have done many, many more other things that I maybe blanked out.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We thought we were safe at the Adams'.
The Adams had trained their children not to tell anybody.
Anybody who walked into our little room had to know the secret code.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we lived there.
We had money enough.
My father gave them money to buy stamps on the black market.
Because it was black market, you know.
You had to have stamps.
They had a family to feed.
But then one day, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my mother and other ones decided it's not safe anymore.
Neighbors are starting to know that they're hiding Jews.
And one of the neighbors, who worked for the Germans, would have loved to tell and have his reward.
And by the way, the reward for the Germans was, if you found a Jew, you got a carton of cigarettes or 200 francs or a bottle of whiskey.
And that was the life worth of a Jew.
That's what you paid-- you got paid for.
It was in-- the, the name of the bakery, I don't remember.
Oh, on the [NON-ENGLISH] Street-- that was the street of the bakery.
The name, I don't remember.
But it wasn't too far, by the way, from our [NON-ENGLISH] Strasse.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Maybe I should show the map of the-- of, of Antwerp.
I have it with me.
And I'll show you the, the bakery.
And it was-- I am sure it was a, a Jewish bakery because nobody was there anymore.
It was empty already.
So the Jews must have run.
No.
The Adams' house was far away.
We walked at night.
See, we weren't allowed to go on trolley cars-- that's Antwerp-- because we were afraid the SS would recognize us.
They would see something strange.
So all it was-- sometimes-- I was hidden in 18 different places.
And sometimes, we went with a car if the Underground had a car.
And if not, we walked.
OK.
We walked.
It was the Gestapo--
--the SS, SS.
Yes, the Adams family, as I said, they were very lovely people.
He was a trolley-car conductor, a very simple person.
They went every Sunday to church.
Mrs.
Adams-- my mother and we all knew that she used to love to drink a little.
And she spent a lot of money on her drinking.
But we didn't care because she was a good-hearted, wonderful person.
And she was very nice to us.
And--
They were taking a risk.
Mr.
Adams could've been in jail until the war is over, if there would have been a war over.
If there would have been real SS men coming for him, they could've shot him, too, take him away to a camp.
And he should never come back.
Every one of the people who hid us, or who hid any Jew, took a risk.
And the risk, even the smallest risk, was a big risk because you had to feel that you're ready to go to jail, at least.
So the people, I feel-- and this I'm saying in general, because mine-- the people who saved us, each one of them has a different story what is very heartfelt.
Well, I think the Adams did it because they were good Catholics.
They really knew what means life.
And they really knew their Ten Commandments. 'Thou shall not kill.' And they felt that if they have children, what would happen if they would go for help?
They would want somebody to help their children.
But I think all the rescuers, those-- they put their life in danger.
And they were the real human beings.
They were the 20th-century human beings.

The other ones didn't understand-- forgot that we are not living in a Stone Age anymore, that we are living some more-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in a world that should be much better than the Stone Age.
With the Adams?
As soon-- well, in '42 we went in hiding-- beginning of '43.
It was Blancha.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had two or three people who helped us, in general.
And they worked with the Underground, with the Antwerp Underground.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Some of them never told us that they were working with the Underground-- until later on, we knew about it.
They were individuals.
They were just good people who knew Jews need help.
Somebody told them.
The Adams must have telled Blancha she needs help.
Or my mother knew Blancha, and she went to ask.
And that's how we got help.
